verses.text: David flees to Jonathan, asking, 'what have I done? what is mine iniquity?' Jonathan can scarcely believe his father means murder, yet vows, 'whatsoever thy soul desireth, I will even do it.' True friendship listens to a hurting heart and binds itself to help.

David proposes a test at the new moon feast and appeals to their covenant: 'deal kindly... for thou hast brought thy servant into a covenant of the LORD with thee.' He rests his hope on a bond made before God. Covenants sealed with God hold when all else shakes.

David proposes a test at the new moon feast and appeals to their covenant: 'deal kindly... for thou hast brought thy servant into a covenant of the LORD with thee.' He rests his hope on a bond made before God. Covenants sealed with God hold when all else shakes.

Jonathan assures David he would never hide such danger from him, and they go out to the field to talk freely. Real friends do not flatter or conceal; they tell each other the truth, even when it is hard. Honesty is the soil where trust grows deep.

Jonathan swears before the LORD to send David word, good or evil, and prays, 'the LORD be with thee, as he hath been with my father.' He blesses the very man who will take the throne his father would keep for him. Love releases its own claim.

Jonathan swears before the LORD to send David word, good or evil, and prays, 'the LORD be with thee, as he hath been with my father.' He blesses the very man who will take the throne his father would keep for him. Love releases its own claim.

Jonathan asks only that David show God's kindness to his house in days to come, and binds the covenant again 'because he loved him as his own soul.' Looking past his own loss, he secures mercy for his children. Selfless love plans for others, not itself.

They arrange the cover story for David's absence and a place for him to hide by the field. Wisdom takes prudent steps even while trusting God. Faith does not forbid careful planning; David hides, waits for Jonathan's sign, and leaves the outcome with the LORD.

They arrange the cover story for David's absence and a place for him to hide by the field. Wisdom takes prudent steps even while trusting God. Faith does not forbid careful planning; David hides, waits for Jonathan's sign, and leaves the outcome with the LORD.

Jonathan devises the sign of the arrows — whether he calls them near or beyond will tell David to stay or flee — and says, 'the LORD be between thee and me for ever.' Their whole friendship rests on God as witness. He is the bond no distance can break.

At the feast David's place is empty, and the first day Saul says nothing, supposing him ceremonially unclean. Evil often bides its time, watching quietly before it strikes. The calm is only the gathering of the storm in a jealous heart.

At the feast David's place is empty, and the first day Saul says nothing, supposing him ceremonially unclean. Evil often bides its time, watching quietly before it strikes. The calm is only the gathering of the storm in a jealous heart.

At the feast David's place is empty, and the first day Saul says nothing, supposing him ceremonially unclean. Evil often bides its time, watching quietly before it strikes. The calm is only the gathering of the storm in a jealous heart.

When David is absent a second day, Saul asks, and Jonathan gives the agreed excuse — David has gone to a family sacrifice. The test is set in motion. Sometimes truth must be guarded carefully from those who would use it to do harm.

When David is absent a second day, Saul asks, and Jonathan gives the agreed excuse — David has gone to a family sacrifice. The test is set in motion. Sometimes truth must be guarded carefully from those who would use it to do harm.

When David is absent a second day, Saul asks, and Jonathan gives the agreed excuse — David has gone to a family sacrifice. The test is set in motion. Sometimes truth must be guarded carefully from those who would use it to do harm.

Saul erupts in fury, shaming Jonathan and his mother, and lays bare his real fear: while David lives, 'thou shalt not be established, nor thy kingdom.' Rage at last speaks the truth jealousy hides — Saul cares for his dynasty more than for God's will.

Saul erupts in fury, shaming Jonathan and his mother, and lays bare his real fear: while David lives, 'thou shalt not be established, nor thy kingdom.' Rage at last speaks the truth jealousy hides — Saul cares for his dynasty more than for God's will.

When Jonathan dares ask, 'what hath he done?', Saul hurls the javelin at his own son. Now Jonathan knows. He leaves the table in fierce anger and grief — not for the insult to himself, but for the wrong done to David. Love feels another's wound as its own.

Next morning Jonathan goes to the field with a boy and shoots the arrow beyond him, calling out the agreed words. The signal of danger is given. A faithful friend keeps his promise exactly, even when keeping it costs him dearly and parts him from the one he loves.

Jonathan hurries the boy, then sends him back with the weapons so none will suspect. He covers David's escape down to the last detail. Quiet, careful faithfulness — the unseen work that protects a friend — is love proven, not merely spoken.

Jonathan hurries the boy, then sends him back with the weapons so none will suspect. He covers David's escape down to the last detail. Quiet, careful faithfulness — the unseen work that protects a friend — is love proven, not merely spoken.

When the boy is gone, David comes out, and the two weep together until David weeps the most. They part with the words, 'the LORD be between me and thee... for ever.' Some of life's dearest bonds end in tearful parting; but a friendship founded on God outlasts every separation.
